content: Consider a lossless transmission line terminated with a short circuit as shown in the figure below. As one moves towards the generator from the load, the normalized impedances $Z_{inA}$, $Z_{inB}$, $Z_{inC}$, and $Z_{inD}$ (indicated in the figure) are ______.
explanation:
Given : Lossless transmission line terminated with short circuit

For a lossless transmission line terminated with short circuit, input impedance is given by

$$ Z_{\text {insc }}=j Z_0 \tan \beta l $$

Normalized impedance is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{\text {insc }}=\frac{Z_{\text {insc }}}{Z_0}=\frac{j Z_0 \tan \beta l}{Z_0} \\ & Z_{\text {insc }}=j \tan \beta l \end{aligned} $$

For normalized impedance $Z_{i n A}$.

Electrical length is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \beta l=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{8}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \tan \beta l=\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

Normalized input impedance at $A$ is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{i n A}=j \tan \frac{\pi}{4} \\ & Z_{i n A}=j 1 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

For normalized impedance $Z_{\text {in } B}$.

Electrical length is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \beta l=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \tan \beta l=\tan \frac{\pi}{2}=\infty \end{aligned} $$

Normalized input impedance at $B$ is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{i n B}=j \tan \frac{\pi}{2} \\ & Z_{i n B}=\infty \end{aligned} $$

For normalized impedance $Z_{\text {in } C}$.

Electrical length is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \beta l=\frac{2 \lambda}{\lambda} \frac{3 \lambda}{8} \\ & \beta l=\frac{3 \pi}{4} \\ & \tan \beta l=\tan \frac{3 \pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

Normalized input impedance at $C$ is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{i n C}=j \tan \frac{3 \pi}{4} \\ & Z_{i n C}=-j 1 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

For normalized impedance $Z_{\text {inD }}$.

Electrical length is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \beta l=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{4 \lambda}{8}=\pi \\ & \tan \beta l=\tan \pi \end{aligned} $$

Normalized input impedance at $D$ is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{i n D}=j \tan \pi \\ & Z_{i n D}=0 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content:
The impedance matching Network shown in figure is to match a lossless line having characteristic impedance $Z_o= 50 \Omega$ with a load impedance $Z_L$. A quarter - Wave line having a characteristic impedance $Z_1=75 \Omega$ is connected to $Z_L$. Two stubs having characteristic impedance of $75 \Omega$ each are connected to this quarter - wave line. One is a short - circuited (S.C) stub of length $0.25 \lambda$ connected across PQ and the other one in an open - Circuted (O.C) stub of length 0.5 $\lambda$ connected across RS.

The impedance matching is achieved when the real part of $Z_L$ is
explanation:
From question, we have

Sc of the length $\lambda / 4 \Rightarrow O C$

OC of length $\lambda / 2 \Rightarrow O C$

$Z_L$ at distance of $\lambda / 4 \Rightarrow$

Then we have new impedance $=\frac{Z^2{ }_1}{Z_L}=\frac{75^2}{Z_L}$

Above is in parallel to OC [Sc modified]

i.e.,   $$ \frac{75^2}{Z_L} / / \mathrm{OC}=\frac{75^2}{Z_L} $$

Above is a match with $Z_o=50 \Omega$

So we have

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{75^2}{Z_L} & =50 \Rightarrow Z_L=\frac{75^2}{50} \\ & =112.5 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

content: A transmission line of length $3 \lambda / 4$ and having a characteristic impedance of $50 \Omega$ is terminated with a load of $400 \Omega$. The impedance (rounded off to two decimal places) seen at the input end of the transmission line is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
explanation:
Given : Length of transmission line, $l=\frac{3 \lambda}{4}$

Characteristic impedance, $Z_0=50 \Omega$

Load of transmission line, $Z_L=400 \Omega$

For a lossless transmission line the input impedance is given by,

$$ Z_{\text {in }}=Z_0\left[\frac{Z_L+j Z_0 \tan \beta \ell}{Z_0+j Z_L \tan \beta \ell}\right] \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

For $\frac{3 \lambda}{4}$ transmission line,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \beta \ell=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{3 \lambda}{4}=\frac{3 \pi}{2}=270^{\circ} \\ & \tan \beta \ell=\tan 270^{\circ}=\frac{\sin 270}{\cos 270}=\frac{-1}{0}=\infty \end{aligned} $$

From, equation (i) $\tan \beta \ell$ will be common from numerator and denominator,

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{\text {in }}=Z_0\left[\frac{\frac{Z_L}{\tan \beta \ell}+j Z_0}{\frac{Z_0}{\tan \beta \ell}+j Z_L}\right] \\ & Z_{\text {in }}=Z_0\left[\frac{\frac{Z_L}{\tan 270}+j Z_0}{\frac{Z_0}{\tan 270}+j Z_L}\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & Z_{\text {in }}=Z_0\left[\frac{0+j Z_0}{0+j Z_L}\right] \\ & Z_{\text {in }}=\frac{Z_0^2}{Z_L} \\ & Z_{\text {in }}=\frac{50^2}{400}=\frac{50 \times 50}{400} \\ & Z_{\text {in }}=6.25 \Omega \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Hence, the impedance seen at the input end of the transmission line is } 6.25 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

content: The impedances $Z=j X$, for all $X$ in the range ( $-\infty, \infty$ ), map to the Smith chart as

options.content:
a circle of radius 1 with centre at $(0,0)$.

xx
a line passing through the centre of the chart.
a circle of radius 0.5 with centre at $(0.5,0)$.
a point at the centre of the chart.

explanation:
Given : Impedance, $Z=j X$

Normalized impedance,

$$ z=\frac{Z}{Z_0}=\frac{j X}{Z_0}=r+j x $$

$$ r=0 \text { and } x=\left(\frac{X}{Z_0}\right) \rightarrow \text { Normalized reactance } $$

We have $X \rightarrow[-\infty, \infty]$

As $r=0 \rightarrow$ fixed, so locus point is on constants resistance circle

Centre $\left\{\frac{r}{1+r}, 0\right\}=\{0,0\}$

Radius $=\frac{1}{1+r}=\frac{1}{1}=1$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

options.content: The signal gets distorted if $${Z_R}\, \ne \,{Z_0}$$ , irrespective of the value of $${Z_T}$$
The signal gets distorted if $${Z_T}\, \ne \,{Z_0}$$, irrespective of the value of $${Z_R}$$
Signal distortion implies impedance mismatch at both ends: $${Z_T}\, \ne \,{Z_0}$$ and $${Z_R}\, \ne \,{Z_0}$$
Impedance mismatches do NOT result in signal distortion but reduce power transfer efficiency

A $50 \Omega$ lossless transmission line is terminated with a load $Z_L$ of $(50-j 75) \Omega$. If the average incident power on the line is 10 mW , then the average power delivered to the load (in mW , rounded off to one decimal place) is _________ .
explanation:
To calculate the average power delivered to the load, we start by determining the reflection coefficient, $\Gamma$, and use it to find the power delivered.

Given Parameters:

Characteristic impedance, $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$

Load impedance, $Z_L = 50 - j75 \, \Omega$

Incident power, $P_{\text{in}} = 10 \, \text{mW}$

Calculate the Reflection Coefficient ($\Gamma$):

$ \Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{(50 - j 75) - 50}{(50 - j 75) + 50} = \frac{-j 75}{100 - j 75} $

Simplify $\Gamma$:

$ \Gamma = \frac{-j 3}{4 - j 3} $

Magnitude of the Reflection Coefficient ($|\Gamma|$):

$ |\Gamma| = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 $

Calculate Power Delivered to the Load ($P_{\text{del}}$):

$ P_{\text{del}} = \left(1 - |\Gamma|^2\right) P_{\text{in}} = \left[1 - (0.6)^2\right] \times 10 \, \text{mW} $

$ P_{\text{del}} = (1 - 0.36) \times 10 \, \text{mW} = 0.64 \times 10 \, \text{mW} = 6.4 \, \text{mW} $

Thus, the average power delivered to the load is $6.4 \, \text{mW}$.

content: A lossless transmission line with characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 50 \Omega$ is terminated with an unknown load. The magnitude of the reflection coefficient is $|\Gamma| = 0.6$. As one moves towards the generator from the load, the maximum value of the input impedance magnitude looking towards the load (in $\Omega$) is _________.
explanation:
Given :

(i) Characteristic impedance $Z_0=50 \Omega$

(ii) Magnitude of reflection coefficient $|\Gamma|=0.6$

Maximum input impedance is given by,

$$ Z_{\max }=Z_0 S $$

where, $S=$ Voltage standing wave ratio

Voltage standing wave ratio is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & S=\frac{1+|\Gamma|}{1-|\Gamma|}=\frac{1+0.6}{1-0.6}=\frac{1.6}{0.4}=4 \\ & Z_{\max }=Z_0 S \\ & Z_{\max }=50 \times 4=200 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the maximum value of input impedance magnitude looking towards the load is $\mathbf{2 0 0} \boldsymbol{\Omega}$.

content: The standing wave ratio on a 50 $$\Omega$$ lossless transmission line terminated in an unknown load impedance is found to be 2.0. The distance between successive voltage minima is 30 cm and the first minimum is located at 10 cm from the load. $$Z_L$$ can be replaced by an equivalent length $$l_m$$ and terminating resistance $$R_m$$ of the same line. The value of $$R_m$$ and $$l_m$$, respectively, are
explanation:
Given : $s=2 \Rightarrow|\Gamma|=\frac{5-1}{5+1}=\frac{1}{3}$ Also, $\frac{\lambda}{2}=30 \Rightarrow \lambda=60 \mathrm{~cm}$

First minima occurs at $Z_{\min }=10 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2 \beta \times Z_{\min }=\pi+\theta_{\Gamma} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 \times 2 \pi}{60} \times 10=\pi+\theta_{\Gamma} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \theta_{\Gamma}=\frac{-\pi}{3} \\ & \therefore \Gamma=\frac{1}{3} \angle-60^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow Z_L=Z_0\left[\frac{1+\Gamma}{1-\Gamma}\right]=67.97 \angle-32.67^0 $$

Now if $Z_L$ is replaced by an equivalent of length $\ell_m$ and terminating resistance $R_m$ then

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_L=Z_0\left[\frac{R_m+j Z_0 \tan \beta \ell_m}{Z_0+j R_m \tan \beta \ell_m}\right] \\\\ & \Rightarrow 67.97\left\lfloor-32.67^0=50\left[\frac{R_m+j 50 \tan \beta \ell_m}{50+j R_m \tan \beta \ell_m}\right]\right. \end{aligned} $$

Only options B and C satisfies the condition (i).

Hence, the correct options are (B) and (C).
